name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Retest Three-CTA Residency There

summary.name: Restore The Compact PTX Hot-Band And Retest Three-CTA Residency There
summary.hypothesis: The regular sibling regression shows that 3-CTA residency is not bad by itself; the bad part is the regular sibling's barrier-heavy consume path. Restoring the accepted compact PTX hot-band launch and then tightening only the PTX kernel launch-bounds target from 2 to 3 CTAs/SM is the cleanest way to test whether the occupancy win can be kept without inheriting the 10.15% barrier tax.
summary.expected_bottleneck: The accepted compact PTX surface is still register-limited, but the beneficial part of the current regression may be the 3-CTA residency rather than the sibling kernel body.
summary.risk: Moderate. This is only a two-part local edit on the accepted PTX surface, but a tighter launch-bounds target can still introduce spills or weaken instruction scheduling.
ranking_notes: This is the best isolation experiment because it keeps the accepted compact PTX surface and imports only the one positive signal from the regressed run: higher CTA residency.
It directly matches the latest delta table: occupancy improved, but barrier and short-scoreboard regressed. This edit is designed to keep the first while avoiding the latter.
It is lower risk than another full sibling swap or a fresh tiling reopen.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if runtime regresses again without a clear barrier reduction, or if build / NCU evidence suggests spill-driven residency forcing.
expected_local_changes: Restore the default fixed-shape hot-band dispatch to the compact PTX microkernel surface.
Retune the PTX hot-band launch-bounds annotation from the current 2-CTA target toward 3-CTA residency.
Leave grouped rows, shared-memory footprint, and stage depth unchanged in the first validation pass.

notes: Node C may now implement this one candidate.
